EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was located near the border region of the mid-Atlantic and New England for much of the day on August 28. South of the front, a hot and humid air mass developed. In addition, a fast moving approaching shortwave disturbance caused shear to increase, creating an environment of moderate to strong shear and instability as well as high moisture. Forcing from the shortwave caused widespread convection to develop by early to mid afternoon. Significant storm organization occurred, particularly in association with a line of storms that impacted portions of Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and northern Delmarva. This mesoscale convective system (MCS) produced widespread wind damage, with a few other storms across the region also producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were downed in Tylersport. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front along with an upper level disturbance approached the mid-Atlantic on August 29. Shear increased as these systems approached. A very humid air mass was in place across the mid-Atlantic, which contributed to considerable cloud cover over the region for much of the day. This limited instability. The approaching front did initiate scattered to widespread convection. Heavy rain ended up being the greater threat than severe weather due to marginal instability, but a few reports of damaging winds were received with stronger storms over Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed near Bradford St. Time estimated from radar.

During the early evening of April 7th, a warm front extended east-southeastward from a surface low centered in west-central Michigan to northwest Pennsylvania. This front moved northeastward and allowed the warm sector to overspread northwest Pennsylvania by late that evening. A cold front extended west-southwestward from the surface low and swept south-southeastward through northwest Pennsylvania during the predawn hours of April 8th before the surface low reached Long Island by daybreak that morning.||Showers and thunderstorms developed in vicinity of the warm front over northwest Pennsylvania during the evening of the 7th and moved generally east-southeastward. A separate squall line in vicinity of the cold front crossed northwest Pennsylvania generally from northwest to southeast during approximately the last hour of the 7th through first hour of the 8th. These storms encountered primarily moderate instability amidst strong effective bulk shear. Severe hail was reported in Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook report and photo of quarter-size hail in Cambridge Springs. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias brought high winds, heavy rain, several tornadoes, and coastal flooding to the mid-Atlantic region, becoming the most impactful tropical cyclone to impact most of the region since Sandy in 2012. Flash flooding as a result of heavy rain caused two direct deaths from this event...one in Lehigh County and the other in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along Valley Creek in the Valley Forge area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weak disturbances and their associated boundaries, in combination with an unstable airmass, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Localized heavy downpours were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed wires near Kline Road and Route 419. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across the mid-Atlantic early in the morning of August 27, stalling over southern New England. A robust mid-level disturbance along with multiple waves of surface low pressure then tracked along the front during the day. Over southern New England and southern New York, a uniquely favorable environment of high wind shear and high instability contributed to a significant severe weather outbreak. Further south, shear and forcing were a bit weaker. However, thunderstorms eventually progressed southward into the mid-Atlantic, fueled in part by outflow from storms to the north. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Chardonnay Dr. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of August 23, bringing some showers with it. After a midday lull in precipitation, a lee side surface trough helped to initiate some thunderstorms in a moist and moderately unstable environment. The relatively weak forcing and limited shear prevented much storm organization, but a few storms did become strong to severe with a few instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Clonmell Upland Rd north of Avondale. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level impulse moved over the mid-Atlantic on August 24. Weak shear prevented storms from becoming well organized, but the instability allowed a couple of storms to become strong to severe and produce a few instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Woodchuck Dr near Trachsville. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to Berks County during the early hours of August 2.  Rainfall totals as high as 4 to 6 inches fell in the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred in Maidencreek Township.  Main Street in Blandon was closed at the railroad tracks.  US Route 222 was under water at Dries Road.  Willow Creek was out of its banks at a number of locations in the township.  A tributary of Willow Creek was also out of its banks and caused flooding on Cornerstone Drive.

The intersection of Darby Avenue and 6th Avenue was under water.  PA Route 12 was closed between PA Route 61 and the 5th Street Highway.  Flooding occurred around the intersection of Hain Avenue and Laurel Run Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across the mid-Atlantic early in the morning of August 27, stalling over southern New England. A robust mid-level disturbance along with multiple waves of surface low pressure then tracked along the front during the day. Over southern New England and southern New York, a uniquely favorable environment of high wind shear and high instability contributed to a significant severe weather outbreak. Further south, shear and forcing were a bit weaker. However, thunderstorms eventually progressed southward into the mid-Atlantic, fueled in part by outflow from storms to the north. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the Bethlehem area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front along with an upper level disturbance approached the mid-Atlantic on August 29. Shear increased as these systems approached. A very humid air mass was in place across the mid-Atlantic, which contributed to considerable cloud cover over the region for much of the day. This limited instability. The approaching front did initiate scattered to widespread convection. Heavy rain ended up being the greater threat than severe weather due to marginal instability, but a few reports of damaging winds were received with stronger storms over Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season Nor'Easter produced anywhere from 6 inches of snow and sleet in the Lower Susquehanna Valley up to 30 inches of snow in parts of Tioga County. Widespread travel impacts were observed, and it was the heaviest snowfall from a single event that many central Pennsylvania locations had seen in a few years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 17 to 32 inches of snow across Tioga County from December 16-17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias brought high winds, heavy rain, several tornadoes, and coastal flooding to the mid-Atlantic region, becoming the most impactful tropical cyclone to impact most of the region since Sandy in 2012. Flash flooding as a result of heavy rain caused two direct deaths from this event...one in Lehigh County and the other in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brookside Road was closed at Buckeye Road in Lower Macungie Township due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley on Monday, April 13 and extended a cold front across northwestern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 13th. With the cold frontal passage, strong winds mixed down to the surface, resulting in wind gusts of 45 to 60 mph across the area. Some tree damage occurred and power lines were downed as a result of the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept east across Lake Erie behind a departing low pressure system during the early morning hours of the 13th. Strong west winds up to 45 knots developed over Lake Erie on Monday. These winds, along with  high lake water levels and waves of 10 to 15 feet, supported high water rises along the Lake Erie shoreline. ||On the afternoon of the 13th, the water level at Erie PA (NOAA/NOS/CO-OPs station 9063038) reached 576.21 feet, or 7.2 feet (87 inches) above normal lake levels. Major flood level for Presque Isle State Park is 576 feet, at which point the peninsula was cut off from the mainland. Park Rangers reported significant beach erosion and subsidence and damage of flooded park roads. Wave run-up on the shoreline and over breakwalls caused severe erosion and minor damage to numerous Erie County shoreline properties from the City of Erie to the Village of North East. Emergency repairs were needed to property seawalls protecting homes. East Dobbins Landing in Erie was underwater with significant property damage to a local business.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After crossing into Philadelphia County, the tornado continued moving northwest through the Philadelphia Mills Mall and then to Knights Road and Medford Road toward Dunks Ferry Road. Three exhaust |systems and 6 RTU systems were blown off the roof in less than 15 |seconds at the former Walmart Store currently undergoing |reconstruction, and numerous trees, some close to 100 years old, |were snapped or blown over along Knights Road, Medford Road, and |Dunks Ferry Road. Roofs, siding, and awnings were blown off many |homes in the area. A few cars were also tossed or turned over as |the storm moved through. The estimated wind speed in this area was|100 mph, equivalent to EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita scale. The tornado may have briefly lifted as it continued northwestward and prepared to cross into Bucks County for a second time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake Erie broke another record high monthly mean in April 2020. April average water levels reached 574.31 feet, which was above the long term record of 574.08 feet in 1985. The lake levels peaked at 574.40 feet on the 8th and 11th.  For the month the lake averaged 32 inches above its monthly long term average, and 9 inches above the previous year���s level. The net basin supply for the month was well below average due to below average precipitation and runoff, however contributions from the record high Lakes St. Clair, and Lakes Michigan-Huron. There were lakeshore flood events on April 13 in Erie Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record lake levels continued to lead to flooding along lakeshore locations from North East to just west of Presque Isle. Beach erosion, flooded marinas, and a few flooded local roads was ongoing throughout April. With strong gales, wind-fueled waves crashed over Presque Isle State Park and impacted  the City of Erie businesses and marinas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado occurred in Worcester Township in Montgomery |County, Pennsylvania as Tropical Storm Isaias moved northward |along the East Coast of the United States. With southeastern |Pennsylvania being in the northeastward, right front quadrant, the|tornado and parent storm moved in an unusual northwestward |trajectory ahead of the main center of the tropical storm. An area|of circulation tightened in a strong cell within a rainband in |southeastern Montgomery County. As this circulation tightened, a |brief tornado formed near the intersection of Brindle Court and |Township Line Road in Worcester Township, where a few small trees |were downed in a small residential subdivision. From here, the |tornado continued its northwestward trajectory through the |township, downing trees along Potshop Road. The circulation may |have lifted with the path being somewhat discontinuous. A somewhat|denser area of trees were downed as the tornado moved across |Valley Forge Road and northwestward along Heebner Road. Several |tree tops were sheared off in addition to several utility poles |being pushed over along Heebner Road near the intersection of |Shefley Lane. The tornado continued northwest from here, |eventually downing several hardwood trees along Hollow Road. One |tree brought down several utility lines as it fell. From here, the|tornado lifted with only a few other trees being downed a little |ways from Hollow Road in a small wooded area.

Numerous trees were snapped |in this area as well, with many homes and cars damaged due to |falling trees and tree limbs. The Sycamore Drive and Harding Roads|areas were hardest hit. The estimated wind speed in this area was|90 mph, equivalent to an EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita scale.| |Current evidence again suggests the tornado lifted one more time |as the parent circulation traveled to the Doylestown area, where |it touched down one final time near the Central Bucks West High |School. Here bleachers on the visitor���s side of the athletic |field were picked up and tossed before the vortex moved on to the |Doylestown Hospital complex where at least six automobiles were |tossed some distance, and where substantial damage occurred to the|Children���s Village Day Care center with no serious injuries |reported. Numerous trees were felled in and around the hospital |center, and several metal lamp posts were bent from the wind. The |estimated wind speed in this area was 115 mph, equivalent to a |low-end EF2 on the Enhanced Fujita scale. The tornado then |continued moving in a northwesterly direction, snapping and |falling trees, as well as causing damage to homes and other |properties, especially along and near Sandy Ridge Road, Jefferson |Avenue, and Lower State Road, before lifting along Ferry Road near|the Peace Valley Reservoir. The estimated wind speed in this area|was 100 mph, equivalent to an EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias brought high winds, heavy rain, several tornadoes, and coastal flooding to the mid-Atlantic region, becoming the most impactful tropical cyclone to impact most of the region since Sandy in 2012. Flash flooding as a result of heavy rain caused two direct deaths from this event...one in Lehigh County and the other in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5-year-old girl with autism was swept away by flood waters running through a tributary of Towamencin Creek after wandering away from her nearby family home.  Rescuers found the victim's body adjacent to the creek in Fischer's Park after a 22-hour search.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak and fast moving wave of low pressure brought a light mix of rain and snow to parts of the mid-Atlantic. Some areas in Pennsylvania received 2 to 3 inches of snowfall, but impacts associated with this system were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 inches of snow fell, including near West Sadsbury and West Caln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing west of the mid-Atlantic and redeveloping near the coast brought a heavy precipitation event to the region. Warmer than average temperatures allowed most of the precipitation to fall as rain. However, some marginally cold air at the onset of the storm led to light icing in portions of the Pennsylvania Poconos and northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell in the higher elevations, with the Mount Pocono ASOS recording 0.30 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes on January 18. Warm advection driven precipitation overspread the mid-Atlantic throughout the day ahead of a warm front. The antecedent air mass was colder than average, allowing precipitation to fall as a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain for a considerable time over parts of the region, despite the area of low pressure tracking so far west and gradually drawing warmer air into the region. Precipitation eventually changed over to mainly rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light mix of snow and freezing rain occurred. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches, and the Reading, PA ASOS (KRDG) measured 0.06 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northern Lower Michigan on the afternoon of January 18th moved east through the Great Lakes region and extended a cold front through Northwest Pennsylvania during the evening hours. With the front, scattered snow showers moved over the region, bringing light accumulations through the morning hours of the 19th. Lake effect snow became more organized during the day on the 19th with northwest flow setting up over the region. A multi-band lake effect snow event developed over the region with a dominant band originating upstream over Lake Huron and this band had snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour. This band swept east to west over Northwest Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 19th before completely exiting just after sunset. The highest snow total for this event was 15 inches in Greenfield Township. Other selected event totals include: 14.5 inches in Greene Township, 13 inches in North East Township, 12 inches in Corry, 11.3 inches at Canadohta Lake, 11 inches in Harborcreek, 10.6 inches at Erie International Airport, 10.3 inches in Cambridge Springs, 8.5 inches in Meadville, and 6.5 inches in Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New record high monthly mean water levels were set on Lakes Michigan-Huron, St. Clair, and Erie in March 2020. All three lakes surpassed their previous records set in 1986. The individual lakes basins experienced precipitation that was near to above average with the Lake Erie basin receiving the most precipitation, which was 121% of average. Water supplies for all of the lakes were above average in March. The higher than average water supply and high lake levels also contributed to outflows being above average. The preliminary outflow estimates through the St. Clair River, Detroit River, Niagara River, and the St. Lawrence River were at record highs for the month of March. There was one lakeshore flood event during the month on March 22nd and 23rd.  Source USACE Detroit District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Erie continued its seasonal rise from February to March, rising about 2 inches to a level of 573.98 feet. This level was a new record high monthly mean water level for March, surpassing its previous record set in 1986 by 3 inches. The March 2020 level was also 12 inches above the March 2019 level and 34 inches above its long term average level. Lack of lake ice supported higher and more repetitive wave action along the shoreline, resulting in continued beach and cliff erosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to southeastern Pennsylvania on August 13.  Rainfall totals were as high as 2 to 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in Buckingham Township.  Several roads were closed including Mill Road, Forest Grove Road, Lower Mountain Road, PA Route 413, New Hope Road and Smith Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak but steadily strengthening low pressure tracked just offshore of the mid-Atlantic coast on March 23, spreading precipitation over the region. Typical for the time of year and a low track so close to the coast, precipitation fell as mainly rain. However, high pressure to the northeast helped hold some marginally cold air in place over some higher elevation areas, allowing some freezing rain to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations and radar data, along with observations from surrounding areas, indicate that freezing rain fell over portions of the county, and that some light accretion likely occurred especially at higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two bands of low-topped showers with a bit of embedded thunder pushed across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening hours of March 3, 2020. This activity occurred ahead of an approaching cold front, and produced small hail and an isolated report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few trees on Old Sixth Ave near Pinecroft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front was accompanied by a narrow line of thunderstorms as it crossed northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of March 20, 2020. An isolated report of wind damage was received from Elk County as this line of storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree across Route 219 near Johnsonburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a cold front moved through Northeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 20th. One of these thunderstorms produced strong winds in southern Luzerne County, knocking down trees and damaging a roof at a school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds tore off a portion of a roof at Valley Elementary-Middle School on Rock Glen Road. Some trees and wires were brought down in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of August 12.  Rainfall amounts were as high as 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue took place on Lincoln Avenue at Bailey Road in Caln Township.  Another water rescue took place on Park Drive at G.O. Carlson Boulevard in Caln Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north through parts of the mid-Atlantic during the late morning and afternoon of March 3. A cold front followed closely behind it in association with strengthening low pressure moving through the eastern Great Lakes. An upper level shortwave was also approaching the area. The cold front moved rapidly through the region during the evening hours. Ahead of it, a weakly unstable but strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Widespread showers and some embedded thunder developed, and while lightning activity was limited the strength of the forcing and shear profiles allowed for some damaging wind gusts to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed in the area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rode up the Ohio Valley during the nighttime and early morning hours of February 12th to the 13th.  An anomalously moist air mass with precipitable water values of near or just over an inch, combined with warm advection and right entrance region jet dynamics, produced widespread precipitation across the region.||The heaviest rainfall was observed south of I-70, where totals of around an inch fell on nearly saturated ground.  This led to several instance of flooded roads and landslides across portions of southeast Ohio into southwest Pennsylvania.  Further north, where warm air was unable to fully penetrate, accumulating snow was observed.  Reports of two to four inches of accumulation were received from many locations to the north of Pittsburgh. A brief period of icing was also noted, but was of little impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2.7 inches of snow was reported by a CoCoRaHS observer near Marienville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rode up the Ohio Valley during the nighttime and early morning hours of February 12th to the 13th.  An anomalously moist air mass with precipitable water values of near or just over an inch, combined with warm advection and right entrance region jet dynamics, produced widespread precipitation across the region.||The heaviest rainfall was observed south of I-70, where totals of around an inch fell on nearly saturated ground.  This led to several instance of flooded roads and landslides across portions of southeast Ohio into southwest Pennsylvania.  Further north, where warm air was unable to fully penetrate, accumulating snow was observed.  Reports of two to four inches of accumulation were received from many locations to the north of Pittsburgh. A brief period of icing was also noted, but was of little impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow were reported, with a social media user reporting 3 inches at West Middlesex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along a nearly stationary boundary draped across the Central Appalachians, and then lifted across the Mid-Atlantic states on February 7th.  The storm system brought rain initially on the 6th, but cold air filled in quickly behind the departing surface low during the nighttime hours, leading to a brief period of mixed precipitation, and then finally a change to all snow. Snow was most widespread during the morning hours, before tapering to more scattered snow showers outside of the ridges during the afternoon.  Snow finally ended in the higher elevations during the evening/overnight hours leading into February 8th.||With the associated upper trough taking on a negative tilt, and with support from coupled jets aloft and strong cold advection, snowfall rates were sufficient to produce several inches of accumulation across much of the forecast area. This system was thus the most significant winter storm of the 2019-2020 season for the NWS Pittsburgh area of responsibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of accumulation was the general range of numerous reports across the county.  The official measurement at the NWS office in Moon was 4.8 inches as of 5 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moved into the Great Lakes on May 29. While lift associated with the front remained well to the west, a pre-frontal trough also developed over central Pennsylvania. This trough helped allow for thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon in a warm and humid air mass. Storm activity waned as it progressed eastward away from the best lift, but a line of strong to severe storms made it into Berks County, producing some strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 to 70 mph winds at the Tri-County Worship Center. A couple reports of downed tree limbs and utility damage were received from the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow shower pattern continued into the 28th of February, thanks to several mechanisms.  Lake-effect snow showers continued in areas north of I-80, where 24-hour snow totals of around three inches were observed in some locations.  Low-level convergence along a weak surface trough produced some snow showers near the I-70 corridor and south, with upslope influence providing additional support for snow along the Allegheny Front.  Three to six inches of accumulation was noted in many of the higher elevation areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24-hour total of 3.8 inches was reported near Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure tracked out of the Ohio Valley and just east of the Great Lakes on February 5 into February 6. However, some light freezing rain occurred mainly in higher elevation areas at the onset of precipitation. Impacts were limited.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain occurred. The Mount Pocono ASOS (KMPO) measured 0.15 inches of ice accretion. Public and spotter reports of light icing were also received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough associated with an approaching cold front overspread northwest Pennsylvania from the northwest during the late morning through early afternoon of May 29th. Well ahead of the front, a squall line moved east-northeastward across northwest Pennsylvania during the early afternoon. A bow echo embedded in this squall line produced straight-line wind damage in portions of Crawford County. These storms occurred in an environment comprised of moderate instability, fairly-steep low-level lapse rates near 7C/km to 7.5C/km, and moderate vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down near Pettis and Smith Roads (i.e. about 5 miles NNW of Cochranton).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of showers and thunderstorms developed well ahead of an approaching cold front in a moderate to high CAPE and moderate shear environment over central PA during the afternoon and evening hours of May 29, 2020. Sporadic wind damage was observed, along with an isolated report of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail and knocked down large tree limbs in Shunk.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto Hergelrode Drive near King Street southwest of Myerstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rode up the Ohio Valley during the nighttime and early morning hours of February 12th to the 13th.  An anomalously moist air mass with precipitable water values of near or just over an inch, combined with warm advection and right entrance region jet dynamics, produced widespread precipitation across the region.||The heaviest rainfall was observed south of I-70, where totals of around an inch fell on nearly saturated ground.  This led to several instance of flooded roads and landslides across portions of southeast Ohio into southwest Pennsylvania.  Further north, where warm air was unable to fully penetrate, accumulating snow was observed.  Reports of two to four inches of accumulation were received from many locations to the north of Pittsburgh. A brief period of icing was also noted, but was of little impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow accumulation were reported, with the high total of 4 inches reported by a social media user at Fisher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of April 9. During the late morning and early afternoon, the mid-Atlantic was in a narrow warm sector with a cold front quickly approaching from the west as low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and then began to redevelop over southern New England. Ahead of the front, a strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed in the area. Pea size hail also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked through southern Canada on April 21. A warm front associated with the low moved through the mid-Atlantic during the morning hours, with a strong and fast moving cold front following closely behind for the afternoon. This was an unseasonably strong cold front which marked the leading edge of a much colder than average air mass moving into the region behind it. A strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed ahead of the front. Widespread morning cloudiness and rain showers limited instability, but a brief window of clearing allowed for heating and destabilization to occur. A severe squall line developed, producing considerable wind damage over eastern portions of the mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the intersection of Reeceville Rd and Swinehart Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad ridging was in place across the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 7th, with the Upper Ohio Valley region experiencing WNW flow aloft.  After morning showers and thunderstorms moved across the area due to a warm front, the upper level flow regime brought an elevated mixed layer (EML) into the region during the afternoon, capping convection during the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Convective clusters over Michigan during the evening congealed into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) and raced across Lake Erie, arriving in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area shortly before midnight.  Steep mid-level lapse rates thanks to the EML allow for a few large hail reports initially.  However, the main impact of the system would be from wind.  Surface temperatures surged into the low to mid 60s ahead of the line on gusty southwest winds, resulting in surface CAPE of 1000 J/kg and a significant curving/lengthening of the hodograph. With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported to be blown down near Buxton Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado path that began near Union Ridge in Columbiana County, Ohio.||A second damage swath coincident with a second radar-detected |circulation and distinct from the Rogers/Negley tornado above was |encountered west along Carmel Achor Road, beginning in the woods |north of the road. This path proceeded south-southeastward with |continuous limb damage and occasional stronger gusts leading to |uprooted trees. Structural damage encountered along this segment |of the path seems to have resulted from falling trees rather than |wind.||The track curved eastward along Pancake Clarkson Road, crossing at|into Beaver County, PA at the Pennsylvania state line, where it |appears to have intensified and widened slightly. In this |location, numerous evergreen trees were snapped or uprooted in the|Sheepskin Hollow State Nature Preserve.||The circulation weakened quickly thereafter, with occasional tree|damage continuing to near Lihi Road and Lisbon Road in Beaver|County before evidence of a path disappeared altogether.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes region on April 30. As it did so, an associated cold front began to approach the coastal mid-Atlantic. A couple waves of low pressure developed along the front. High pressure to the northeast helped contribute to a tight pressure gradient over the region, and a strong southerly low level jet developed. As temperatures warmed during the late morning and afternoon, increased mixing led to strong wind gusts. In some cases, winds were strong enough to produce tree and utility damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some of the most significant impacts were felt in Berks County. Around two dozen reports of downed trees or wires were received from across the county. The Reading Regional Airport (KRDG) ASOS measured a 56 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of April 9. During the late morning and early afternoon, the mid-Atlantic was in a narrow warm sector with a cold front quickly approaching from the west as low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and then began to redevelop over southern New England. Ahead of the front, a strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad ridging was in place across the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 7th, with the Upper Ohio Valley region experiencing WNW flow aloft.  After morning showers and thunderstorms moved across the area due to a warm front, the upper level flow regime brought an elevated mixed layer (EML) into the region during the afternoon, capping convection during the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Convective clusters over Michigan during the evening congealed into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) and raced across Lake Erie, arriving in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area shortly before midnight.  Steep mid-level lapse rates thanks to the EML allow for a few large hail reports initially.  However, the main impact of the system would be from wind.  Surface temperatures surged into the low to mid 60s ahead of the line on gusty southwest winds, resulting in surface CAPE of 1000 J/kg and a significant curving/lengthening of the hodograph. With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported to be blown down along Route 62.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked through southern Canada on April 21. A warm front associated with the low moved through the mid-Atlantic during the morning hours, with a strong and fast moving cold front following closely behind for the afternoon. This was an unseasonably strong cold front which marked the leading edge of a much colder than average air mass moving into the region behind it. A strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed ahead of the front. Widespread morning cloudiness and rain showers limited instability, but a brief window of clearing allowed for heating and destabilization to occur. A severe squall line developed, producing considerable wind damage over eastern portions of the mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in the area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the mid-Mississippi Valley and across the Ohio Valley on April 17th.  Good frontogenetical forcing produced steady precipitation over Pennsylvania.  While most areas started as rain, or turned quickly over from snow to rain, some areas north of I-80 remained cold enough for snow to continue during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several inches were able to accumulate in Forest and Venango Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers near Cooperstown, Titusville, and Seneca reported 3 to 4 inches of snow accumulation during the event.

Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported to be downed in Elizabeth.  One playhouse was blown over 100 yards away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of April 9. During the late morning and early afternoon, the mid-Atlantic was in a narrow warm sector with a cold front quickly approaching from the west as low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and then began to redevelop over southern New England. Ahead of the front, a strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad ridging was in place across the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 7th, with the Upper Ohio Valley region experiencing WNW flow aloft.  After morning showers and thunderstorms moved across the area due to a warm front, the upper level flow regime brought an elevated mixed layer (EML) into the region during the afternoon, capping convection during the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Convective clusters over Michigan during the evening congealed into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) and raced across Lake Erie, arriving in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area shortly before midnight.  Steep mid-level lapse rates thanks to the EML allow for a few large hail reports initially.  However, the main impact of the system would be from wind.  Surface temperatures surged into the low to mid 60s ahead of the line on gusty southwest winds, resulting in surface CAPE of 1000 J/kg and a significant curving/lengthening of the hodograph. With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to be blown down on a driveway.

The last noted tree damage was along Leechburg Road north of Tarentum Road, at which point the circulation appears to have weakened below the damaging threshold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of April 9. During the late morning and early afternoon, the mid-Atlantic was in a narrow warm sector with a cold front quickly approaching from the west as low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and then began to redevelop over southern New England. Ahead of the front, a strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Laurel Heights Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the late morning and afternoon of April 13, 2020, generating two distinct lines of convection, along with a handful of cells ahead of these lines. These storms tapped into a strong low-level jet to produce scattered wind damage across the southern two-thirds of central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near Zimmerman.

With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large commercial garage was reported to be blown off of its foundation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to be downed on a home near Beaver County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad ridging was in place across the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 7th, with the Upper Ohio Valley region experiencing WNW flow aloft.  After morning showers and thunderstorms moved across the area due to a warm front, the upper level flow regime brought an elevated mixed layer (EML) into the region during the afternoon, capping convection during the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Convective clusters over Michigan during the evening congealed into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) and raced across Lake Erie, arriving in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area shortly before midnight.  Steep mid-level lapse rates thanks to the EML allow for a few large hail reports initially.  However, the main impact of the system would be from wind.  Surface temperatures surged into the low to mid 60s ahead of the line on gusty southwest winds, resulting in surface CAPE of 1000 J/kg and a significant curving/lengthening of the hodograph. With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported to be blown down across the area of Sewickley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of April 9. During the late morning and early afternoon, the mid-Atlantic was in a narrow warm sector with a cold front quickly approaching from the west as low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and then began to redevelop over southern New England. Ahead of the front, a strongly forced and highly sheared environment existed. Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees and wires in the area. Time estimated from radar.

Heating and moisture were limited in the small warm sector region, but sufficient instability developed to support convective development, and a broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, causing a considerable amount of wind damage.||That evening, additional severe weather occurred as several post-frontal squalls developed. One of these squalls developed into a low-topped thunderstorm. Steep lapse rates and strong wind profiles allowed this storm to also produce wind damage over southeastern Pennsylvania, northeast Maryland, northern Delaware, and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole was snapped in half in Boyertown. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from an approaching weather system persisted for several hours. A locally enhanced line of thunderstorm activity triggered rapid and enhanced flooding in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Branch of Towanda Creek was over its banks and flooding structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: PA Route 14 is completely flooded over in multiple locations along Towanda Creek extending from upstream into Canton Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from an approaching weather system persisted for several hours. A locally enhanced line of thunderstorm activity triggered rapid and enhanced flooding in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads are flooded around Litchfield with water encroaching into residential areas. Some roads were damaged and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad ridging was in place across the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 7th, with the Upper Ohio Valley region experiencing WNW flow aloft.  After morning showers and thunderstorms moved across the area due to a warm front, the upper level flow regime brought an elevated mixed layer (EML) into the region during the afternoon, capping convection during the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Convective clusters over Michigan during the evening congealed into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) and raced across Lake Erie, arriving in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area shortly before midnight.  Steep mid-level lapse rates thanks to the EML allow for a few large hail reports initially.  However, the main impact of the system would be from wind.  Surface temperatures surged into the low to mid 60s ahead of the line on gusty southwest winds, resulting in surface CAPE of 1000 J/kg and a significant curving/lengthening of the hodograph. With the boundary layer no longer decoupled, strong ambient mid-level flow combined with highly efficient evaporational cooling in the convective downdrafts allowed substantial downdraft CAPE to be realized with surface wind gusts exceeding 70 mph observed and evident over a wide area given the volume of wind damage reports.  Embedded vortices in the quasi-linear convective system also led to five tornadoes in the EF0/EF1 range during this event across the region.||Numerous instances of tree and structural damage were observed, including damage to planes and hangars at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.  Strong winds also tore off part of the roof of St. Mary of Czestochowa Church on Kenneth Avenue in New Kensington. Over 50,000 area electrical customers were without power in the wake of the storms, according to the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was documented along Riverview Street in |Lower Burrell. A narrow path consisting mainly of snapped and |uprooted trees and limbs was encountered. Additionally, modest |damage to shingles and fences was noted. Supporting the presence |of a strong circulation, splatter of mud, pine needles and debris |was discovered on the east-facing side of affected homes, and |downwind lawn furniture and trampolines were lofted and deposited |upwind from their initial locations.||Wind was fairly focused, with damage consistent with 70 mph wind |gusts. This brief tornado was associated with a second |mesovortex/updraft distinct from the Tarentum/New Kensington |tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesovortex intensified rapidly and produced a tornado as its |parent storm descended the terrain northwest of Tarentum into the |Allegheny River valley. The tornado began in the woods between |Bakerstown Road and Days Run Road and rapidly intensified near |Days Run Road and Holsing Lane, where a vast number of softwood |and hardwood trees were snapped, blocking Days Run Road, and with |some falling onto homes. Additionally in this location, windows in|a home were blown in by the tremendous wind. ||A swath of tree damage, likely to be the result of straight-line |wind, extends northwest from the Holsing Road area where it |intersects the tornado damage track. This wind likely was just as |strong (if not stronger) than the tornado itself. ||The tornado slowly weakened as the storm continued southeastward, |with a path of snapped limbs and occasional trunks continuing to |along Bakerstown Road near the Broadview section before continuing|across Route 28. Modest building/carport damage was reported in |Tarentum along Sixth Ave coincident with the passage of the |weakening circulation.  The tornado then crossed the Allegheny River into Westmoreland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred behind an exiting cold front on March 28th. The 12Z morning sounding from PIT showed a strong low-level jet of 55kts near 1500 meters. A saturated soil from storms the previous day may have helped promote falling trees as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mother and toddler were critically injured when a pine tree fell on them in Cascade Park. The falling tree caused them to fall into nearby water. Article: https://www.ncnewsonline.com/news/local_news/man-performed-cpr-on-child-hit-by-tree/article_34f9b0a4-d832-53b1-b405-694fc734b469.html .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northern Lower Michigan on the afternoon of January 18th moved east through the Great Lakes region and extended a cold front through Northwest Pennsylvania during the evening hours. With the front, scattered snow showers moved over the region, bringing light accumulations through the morning hours of the 19th. Lake effect snow became more organized during the day on the 19th with northwest flow setting up over the region. A multi-band lake effect snow event developed over the region with a dominant band originating upstream over Lake Huron and this band had snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour. This band swept east to west over Northwest Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 19th before completely exiting just after sunset. The highest snow total for this event was 15 inches in Greenfield Township. Other selected event totals include: 14.5 inches in Greene Township, 13 inches in North East Township, 12 inches in Corry, 11.3 inches at Canadohta Lake, 11 inches in Harborcreek, 10.6 inches at Erie International Airport, 10.3 inches in Cambridge Springs, 8.5 inches in Meadville, and 6.5 inches in Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northern Lower Michigan on the afternoon of January 18th moved east through the Great Lakes region and extended a cold front through Northwest Pennsylvania during the evening hours. The highest snow total for this event was 15 inches in Greenfield Township. Other selected event totals include: 14.5 inches in Greene Township, 13 inches in North East Township, 12 inches in Corry, 10.5 inches in Edinboro, and 8.1 inches in Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection above the boundary layer the morning of January 18th created mixed precipitation and snow for some areas as a low pressure dug over the Great Plains. Freezing rain potential lingered over the higher terrain per dynamic cooling via upsloping. The heaviest snow fell north and west of Pittsburgh due to sufficient frontogenesis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and freezing rain was reported across Allegheny County the morning of January 18th. A little over an inch of snow was measured near Bridgeville, PA and a trace of freezing rain was measured as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes region on April 30. As it did so, an associated cold front began to approach the coastal mid-Atlantic. A couple waves of low pressure developed along the front. High pressure to the northeast helped contribute to a tight pressure gradient over the region, and a strong southerly low level jet developed. As temperatures warmed during the late morning and afternoon, increased mixing led to strong wind gusts. In some cases, winds were strong enough to produce tree and utility damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Delaware Environmental Observing System (DEOS) gauge near Londonerry Twp measured a 60 mph wind gust. Several reports of downed trees and wires were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 850 mb, a potent low moved from near the northern Indiana/southwest Michigan border on the early evening of January 11th to northern Maine by early afternoon of January 12th. This low was accompanied by a strong 850 mb wind field of about 40 to 60 knots over northwest Pennsylvania from the early evening of the 11th through most of the morning of the 12th. At the surface, a low moved northeastward along a stationary front as the low shifted from north-central Kentucky on the early evening of the 11th to the Gulf of Maine by early afternoon on the 12th. This low's trailing cold front swept eastward over northwest Pennsylvania during the predawn hours of the 12th. A tight pressure gradient accompanying this low pressure system and/or rain showers transporting stronger winds aloft to the surface contributed to high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several trees down in county. Time estimated via radar with passing rain showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be down on Brookwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system moved through Northeast Pennsylvania on the 11th, bringing some showers and thunderstorms to the area. A cold pool aloft resulted in some severe hail in Susquehanna County, PA during the mid afternoon.

A strong low pressure system moved across the central Great Lakes and into southern Canada on April 13th. A 60-70 knot low-level southeasterly jet developed during the early morning hours that day. Winds down-sloped off the Pocono plateau, reaching very high speeds in Lackawanna and Luzerne counties in Northeast Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts during the mid to late morning hours ranged between 60 and 75 mph. These high winds caused structural damage in the Wilkes-Barre area.

A strong low pressure system moved across the central Great Lakes and into southern Canada on April 13th. A 60-70 knot low-level southeasterly jet developed during the early morning hours that day. Winds down-sloped off the Pocono plateau, reaching very high speeds in Lackawanna and Luzerne counties in Northeast Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts during the mid to late morning hours ranged between 60 and 75 mph. These high winds caused structural damage in the Wilkes-Barre area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused significant roof damage to the Wilkes-Barre City Hall. Also, a pedestrian bridge that connects Wilkes-Barre General Hospital with the Outpatient Surgery Center sustained wind damage. Measured gusts in other areas of Luzerne County were as high as 66 mph, including a 64 mph ASOS wind gust at the Wilkes-Barre/Scranton Airport at 829 AM EDT/729 AM LST. This measurement occurred right before the ASOS wind sensor failed there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system crossed the Great Lake region on March 20th and promoted heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. As mid-level cool air advanced into the region by late morning, instability increased and helped promote damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban street flooding and flooding due to swollen creeks affected areas across Beaver County and northwestern Allegheny County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving out of the Southeast began to explosively intensify as it moved over the mid-Atlantic. On the morning of February 7, the eastern mid-Atlantic was briefly within the warm sector of the deepening low. An environment of extreme wind fields was present, and temperatures and dew points rose enough for sufficient instability to develop to sustain convection. A line of low topped but intense convection developed, and despite producing little thunder and lightning it produced a long swath of wind damage over the mid-Atlantic, along with a few tornadoes between Virginia and Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were downed near Manor Rd and Horseshoe Pike. Strong winds occurred well in advance of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 26th moved northeast across the central Appalachians, bringing some system snowfall to the region on the night of the 26th into the 27th. As low pressure departed the eastern Great Lakes region on the 27th, wrap-around cold air allowed for lake effect snow to develop over the mostly ice-free waters of the Great Lakes. Multi-banded lake effect snow from Lakes Erie and Huron developed in the northwest flow over the region and the shallow nature of the snow allowed for light snow accumulations on the 27th and the first half of the 28th. Lake effect snow became more organized and convective in-nature on the 28th and by the evening hours, a Lake Huron connected band focused in on Northwest Pennsylvania, allowing for a period of 12 hours of moderate-to-heavy snowfall. Snow finally diminished by the late morning hours of the 29th. While snow occurred for three and a half days over the region, much of the snowfall occurred on the evening of the 28th through the morning of the 29th in Northwest Pennsylvania. The highest snow total for this event was 21.5 inches in Corry. Other selected event totals include: 20.9 inches in Cambridge Springs, 20 inches in Edinboro, 19.3 inches in Union City, 16 inches in Greenfield and North East Townships, 15 inches in Greene Township, 12.2 inches in Amity Township, 11.5 inches in Meadville, and 10.4 inches in Springboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 26th moved northeast across the central Appalachians, bringing some system snowfall to the region on the night of the 26th into the 27th. Local news reported water over Dobbins Landing and several marinas in Presque Isle and the shoreline of the City of Erie. Minor flooding and beach erosion occurred in Presque Isle State Park. ||On the afternoon and evening of the 27th, the water level at Erie PA (NOAA/NOS/CO-OPs station 9063038) reached 575.49 feet, or 6.5 feet (78 inches) above normal lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving out of the Southeast began to explosively intensify as it moved over the mid-Atlantic. On the morning of February 7, the eastern mid-Atlantic was briefly within the warm sector of the deepening low. An environment of extreme wind fields was present, and temperatures and dew points rose enough for sufficient instability to develop to sustain convection. A line of low topped but intense convection developed, and despite producing little thunder and lightning it produced a long swath of wind damage over the mid-Atlantic, along with a few tornadoes between Virginia and Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of power lines down in the Croydon area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the central Appalachians on the 7th moved northeast towards the New England states. On the back side of this low pressure system, a wintry mix of precipitation set up over Northwest Pennsylvania with snow and freezing drizzle, which transitioned completely to snow by late morning on the 7th. Cold air surged over the region behind the low and snow became more convective with periods of moderate snow with visibility below one mile. These bursts of snow continued through the afternoon hours of the 7th, as synoptic snow transitioned to mostly lake effect and shallow multi-bands set up over the area. As west flow set up over the region late on the 7th, a single lake effect snow band set up over the region, bringing snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour, as it lifted north across the area. Another heavy lake effect snow band developed over Northwest Pennsylvania before dawn on the 8th bringing another window of significant snowfall. The highest snow total for this short event was 15 inches in Greene Township. Other selected event totals include: 14.3 inches in Amity Township, 13.5 inches in North East Township, 13 inches in Edinboro, 12.6 inches in Springboro, 12 inches in Cambridge Springs, 11 inches in Greenfield Township, 8.7 inches in Union City, 7.5 inches in Millcreek Township, and 7.2 inches in Meadville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through the mid-Atlantic as weak low pressure tracked just west of the region. Overrunning precipitation occurred as the front moved through, but warm air advection on top of an already marginally cold air mass yielded mainly liquid precipitation. However, some freezing rain did occur initially in higher elevation areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred, mainly in higher elevations. The Mount Pocono ASOS (KMPO) measured 0.34 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a mid-morning severe weather outbreak, the weather remained active over the mid-Atlantic into the later morning and afternoon hours on February 7. Explosively intensifying low pressure began to pull away from the region to the north, leading to a cold frontal passage. Rapid height and pressure rises on the back side of the departing low led to a period of strong and in some cases damaging synoptic winds following the damaging convective winds from earlier in the day. Winds were strongest in coastal areas. Winds began to diminish late in the day as the low moved further away and the gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Philadelphia Northeast Airport.

Other areas in Northeast PA received less snow as temperatures were warmer and rain mixed in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake Erie continued its seasonal rise in February, rising 4 inches from January to February to a level of 573.82 feet. This level was a new record high monthly mean water level for February, surpassing its previous record set in 1987 by 5 inches. The level was also 11 inches above the February 2019 level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Month Event: Record Lake Erie water levels for February resulted in continued and exasperated lakeshore flooding and erosion. There were two storm events that brought lakeshore flooding to the western basin on the 5th and 6th, and the other on the 27th.  In addition to these wind driven events, weakened shorelines showed signs of collapse and erosion throughout the month. Erosion was made worse by the lack of ice over the winter. Lake Erie reported only .4 percent of ice coverage compared to the average of 67 percent in mid February.  The lack of ice supported increased wave run-up, eroding away cliffs, washing out beaches and damaging shoreline infrastructure. Docks were underwater, creating the risk that boaters could drive over them, ruining their watercraft. Of note substantial erosion has occurred across Presque Isle State Park periodically cutting off access roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving backdoor cold front drifted southwest through the mid-Atlantic region on July 6. Meanwhile, a shortwave vorticity impulse, one of several in a series, moved across the region during the day. A very hot and humid air mass was present ahead of the backdoor cold front, leading to the development of strong to extreme instability over the region. The approaching upper level disturbance allowed for the development of moderate wind shear over the region. Combined with the front, it also acted as a trigger mechanism for convection. Severe thunderstorms developed by early afternoon over eastern PA and southern and central New Jersey. These storms produced strong outflow boundaries which served to initiate additional severe convection. The result was a ring of fire in which outflow steadily propagated outward in all directions. This caused severe weather to spread radially outward from its origin near Philadelphia to much of New Jersey, other portions of eastern Pennsylvania, and Delmarva.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media with photos of hail up to about golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary was draped across upstate New York and southern New England on July 22 with multiple waves of low pressure tracking along it. The mid-Atlantic was left in a warm sector air mass south of this front. This led to a very hot and humid day on July 22 with air temperatures rising into the 90s and dew point values near 70. This caused strong instability to develop. Shear values were not overly impressive, but an approaching shortwave disturbance from the Midwest did help to increase shear late in the day. This disturbance also served as forcing for convection to develop in the warm and unstable air mass. Widespread thunderstorm development occurred, with storms eventually developing into a mostly solid squall line. This line of storms produced numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Delmarva.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed near Hopewell Rd near the border of Berks and Chester Counties. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving backdoor cold front drifted southwest through the mid-Atlantic region on July 6. Meanwhile, a shortwave vorticity impulse, one of several in a series, moved across the region during the day. A very hot and humid air mass was present ahead of the backdoor cold front, leading to the development of strong to extreme instability over the region. The approaching upper level disturbance allowed for the development of moderate wind shear over the region. Combined with the front, it also acted as a trigger mechanism for convection. Severe thunderstorms developed by early afternoon over eastern PA and southern and central New Jersey. These storms produced strong outflow boundaries which served to initiate additional severe convection. The result was a ring of fire in which outflow steadily propagated outward in all directions. This caused severe weather to spread radially outward from its origin near Philadelphia to much of New Jersey, other portions of eastern Pennsylvania, and Delmarva.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter Network report of quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary which contributed to widespread severe weather on July 22 moved further north of the mid-Atlantic on July 23. However, another cold front began to approach from the northwest out of the Great Lakes, along with multiple fast moving upper level disturbances. The environment ahead of the front was characterized by moderate to strong instability, weak to moderate shear, and high moisture. The greatest forcing and lift from the approaching disturbances remained north of the region over southern New England, but thunderstorms still developed over the mid-Atlantic. With plenty of instability to work with some storms became severe mainly as clusters or line segments producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on US-1 near the Route 896 interchange, Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was draped in the area of southern New Jersey and Delmarva on July 30. Meanwhile, another cold front was approaching the region from the northwest. A warm and very humid air mass developed ahead of this second front, leading to the development of moderate to strong instability over much of the mid-Atlantic. The approach of the front also gradually increased wind shear values, though greater shear remained further north over New York and New England. Nonetheless, frontal forcing in the unstable air mass was sufficient to trigger scattered thunderstorms, some of which became strong to severe and produced a number of reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the intersection of Poplar St and Astor St in Norristown. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Burgettstown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported in Peter Township area.

The mid-Atlantic was left in a warm sector air mass south of this front. This led to a very hot and humid day on July 22 with air temperatures rising into the 90s and dew point values near 70. This caused strong instability to develop. Shear values were not overly impressive, but an approaching shortwave disturbance from the Midwest did help to increase shear late in the day. This disturbance also served as forcing for convection to develop in the warm and unstable air mass. Widespread thunderstorm development occurred, with storms eventually developing into a mostly solid squall line. This line of storms produced numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Delmarva.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were downed on Camelback Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few air mass thunderstorms developed over Pennsylvania on July 7. A lack of forcing and little shear limited storm coverage despite a moist and moderately unstable air mass. In addition, the atmosphere had likely not fully recovered from an outbreak of thunderstorms the prior day. Nonetheless, isolated damaging wind occurred with a couple of stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and wires were downed near Route 191 in Coolbaugh Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving backdoor cold front drifted southwest through the mid-Atlantic region on July 6. Meanwhile, a shortwave vorticity impulse, one of several in a series, moved across the region during the day. A very hot and humid air mass was present ahead of the backdoor cold front, leading to the development of strong to extreme instability over the region. The approaching upper level disturbance allowed for the development of moderate wind shear over the region. Combined with the front, it also acted as a trigger mechanism for convection. Severe thunderstorms developed by early afternoon over eastern PA and southern and central New Jersey. These storms produced strong outflow boundaries which served to initiate additional severe convection. The result was a ring of fire in which outflow steadily propagated outward in all directions. This caused severe weather to spread radially outward from its origin near Philadelphia to much of New Jersey, other portions of eastern Pennsylvania, and Delmarva.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of half dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the intersection of Babcock Blvd and Wildwood Rd, the road was completely under water due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front promoted heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon of March 28th. Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and half inch hail reported by an emergency manager; picture provided.

Substantial breaks in the clouds south of the boundary, along with substantial warm air advection, resulted in around 1500 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE,  and substantial (60kts+) effective shear was in place across the region. Hail growth increased into the evening as effective wind shear increased to 70kts over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported via Facebook West of Bessemer.
