EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal entered the western Great Lakes region on June 9th, extending a warm front across the Ohio Valley during the evening hours. A hot, moist, and tropical air mass entered the region behind the front for June 10th, resulting in high temperatures in the upper 80s and dew points in the 70s across Northwest Pennsylvania. A shortwave trough and associated surface cold front entered from the west, allowing for showers and thunderstorms to develop over Ohio during the afternoon hours on the 10th in the warm, unstable air mass. Storms over Ohio morphed into a squall line and reached Northwest Pennsylvania after dark on the 10th. This squall line produced damaging wind gusts in Erie and Crawford Counties with estimated wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph. Crawford County was hit hardest during the event with widespread tree damage noted in the western half of the county, including several falling onto cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Erie County Sheriff Dispatch reported trees down across Lunger Road in Harborcreek Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crawford County Sheriff Dispatch reported widespread tree damage across western portions of Crawford County. A large tree was downed in the Jamestown Campgrounds on the southwest portions of Pymatuning Reservoir. More tree damage was noted in the Linesville Area, North Shenango Township, East Fallowfield Township, and Summit Township. Several roads were closed in the southwest portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed in Northeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd ahead of a passing cold front. These storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew off a 40 by 140 foot warehouse roof and dropped it in a road. The building also collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis lifted quickly across the Great Lakes and the Upper Ohio Valley on November 15th, taking on a negative tilt as it did so. Deepening surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into Ontario during the day, whipping a cold front across the region during the late morning and early afternoon. Some of the classic ingredients for a widespread wind event were present, including a negatively- tilted trough, jet positioning over the region, and a favorable Great Lakes surface low track. Strong post-frontal 3 hour pressure rises of 5-7 mb were observed, and a low-topped mostly lightning-free convective line accompanied the boundary. Widespread wind gusts of at least 45-55 MPH were observed, with several higher measured gusts seen. Along the convective line, gusts of 55-65 MPH likely occurred. This resulted in widespread reports of wind damage to trees and power lines, with a few instances of mostly minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down between Tionesta and Fryburg, with some power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms, some of them severe, moved from northwest to southeast across southeastern Pennsylvania during the mid-morning hours on Wednesday, June 3rd.  Then a derecho, which developed just southeast of Lake Erie during the early morning hours, moved rapidly southeast across Pennsylvania before exiting off the New Jersey coast by early afternoon (130 PM). Damaging winds associated with this derecho were sporadic over western and central Pennsylvania, but as the thunderstorm complex moved into increasingly unstable air in the eastern part of the state just before noon, wind damage reports became more numerous and widespread.  Most of these reports were confined within a 50-mile wide swath extending from Berks County eastward to the Philadelphia metro area, then further east to the Ocean County shoreline in New Jersey.  Wind gust reports between 60 and 70 MPH were common within this swath, with some of the highest gusts as follows:  83 MPH at Reading Regional Airport (Berks County), 76 MPH in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 75 MPH in Malvern (Chester County), and 71 MPH in the Holmesburg section of Philadelphia.  In addition to these destructive wind gusts, frequent to continuous cloud to ground lightning, heavy rain, and a few instances of hail were also reported throughout the area.  ||Tragically, four deaths were reported in eastern Pennsylvania as a result of these severe wind gusts.  Three fatalities in Montgomery County were due directly to falling trees.  The fourth fatality in Delaware County was due to a house fire caused by an electrical malfunction from a fallen tree.  This is the highest number of fatalities from a derecho event in Pennsylvania since 1950.  ||Over 208,000 power outages were reported in southeastern Pennsylvania as a result |of fallen trees on power lines.  Some localities were without power for several days. Numerous road closures were also reported due to fallen trees.|A seven story 150 unit apartment building took severe roof damage in Lansdale Borough, Montgomery County. The Montgomery County Community Based COVID-19 Testing Site shut down at noon due to the storms. Personnel took shelter and reported minor property damage.||Because this derecho moved off the coast by 130 PM, the warm afternoon sun was able to sufficiently destabilize the atmosphere for the formation of another round of severe thunderstorms over some of the same areas that experience them earlier in the day.  Reported wind gusts associated with these thunderstorms generally ranged between 45 and 65 MPH, with a 65 MPH wind gust measured at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The remaining thunderstorms then moved offshore by 1000 PM, which brought an end to the day's severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees snapped in Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate severe weather events affected central Pennsylvania on June 3, 2020. The first event occurred during the pre-dawn hours, as strong thunderstorms pushed southward out of the Buffalo, NY area into the northwest mountains and produced wind damage before dissipating. The second event comprised of an MCS that dropped quickly southeastward across the area during the late morning hours and produced occasional wind damage along it's path. The third event saw scattered thunderstorms, including one long-lived supercell, develop during the afternoon hours and drift southeastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees onto wires southwest of Narvon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal entered the western Great Lakes region on June 9th, extending a warm front across the Ohio Valley during the evening hours. A hot, moist, and tropical air mass entered the region behind the front for June 10th, resulting in high temperatures in the upper 80s and dew points in the 70s across Northwest Pennsylvania. A shortwave trough and associated surface cold front entered from the west, allowing for showers and thunderstorms to develop over Ohio during the afternoon hours on the 10th in the warm, unstable air mass. Storms over Ohio morphed into a squall line and reached Northwest Pennsylvania after dark on the 10th. This squall line produced damaging wind gusts in Erie and Crawford Counties with estimated wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph. Crawford County was hit hardest during the event with widespread tree damage noted in the western half of the county, including several falling onto cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crawford County Sheriff Dispatch reported several trees down in the Conneautville area, including several trees down on vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed in Northeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd ahead of a passing cold front. These storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down numerous large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed on the higher terrain of the Allegheny Front during the early afternoon hours and drifted eastward through the afternoon and early evening in a moderate CAPE, moderate low-level shear environment well ahead of an approaching trough of low pressure over the Great Lakes. A few of these storms produced wind damage, along with an isolated report of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated at 60 mph caused trees to fall on wires near Trout Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed on the higher terrain of the Allegheny Front during the early afternoon hours and drifted eastward through the afternoon and early evening in a moderate CAPE, moderate low-level shear environment well ahead of an approaching trough of low pressure over the Great Lakes. A few of these storms produced wind damage, along with an isolated report of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph downed trees at 3507 McAlevys Fort Road, Huntingdon and also caused trees to fall on wires on Alan Seeger Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis lifted quickly across the Great Lakes and the Upper Ohio Valley on November 15th, taking on a negative tilt as it did so. Deepening surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into Ontario during the day, whipping a cold front across the region during the late morning and early afternoon. Some of the classic ingredients for a widespread wind event were present, including a negatively- tilted trough, jet positioning over the region, and a favorable Great Lakes surface low track. Strong post-frontal 3 hour pressure rises of 5-7 mb were observed, and a low-topped mostly lightning-free convective line accompanied the boundary. Widespread wind gusts of at least 45-55 MPH were observed, with several higher measured gusts seen. Along the convective line, gusts of 55-65 MPH likely occurred. This resulted in widespread reports of wind damage to trees and power lines, with a few instances of mostly minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported to be blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms, some of them severe, moved from northwest to southeast across southeastern Pennsylvania during the mid-morning hours on Wednesday, June 3rd.  Then a derecho, which developed just southeast of Lake Erie during the early morning hours, moved rapidly southeast across Pennsylvania before exiting off the New Jersey coast by early afternoon (130 PM). Damaging winds associated with this derecho were sporadic over western and central Pennsylvania, but as the thunderstorm complex moved into increasingly unstable air in the eastern part of the state just before noon, wind damage reports became more numerous and widespread.  Most of these reports were confined within a 50-mile wide swath extending from Berks County eastward to the Philadelphia metro area, then further east to the Ocean County shoreline in New Jersey.  Wind gust reports between 60 and 70 MPH were common within this swath, with some of the highest gusts as follows:  83 MPH at Reading Regional Airport (Berks County), 76 MPH in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 75 MPH in Malvern (Chester County), and 71 MPH in the Holmesburg section of Philadelphia.  In addition to these destructive wind gusts, frequent to continuous cloud to ground lightning, heavy rain, and a few instances of hail were also reported throughout the area.  ||Tragically, four deaths were reported in eastern Pennsylvania as a result of these severe wind gusts.  Three fatalities in Montgomery County were due directly to falling trees.  The fourth fatality in Delaware County was due to a house fire caused by an electrical malfunction from a fallen tree.  This is the highest number of fatalities from a derecho event in Pennsylvania since 1950.  ||Over 208,000 power outages were reported in southeastern Pennsylvania as a result |of fallen trees on power lines.  Some localities were without power for several days. Numerous road closures were also reported due to fallen trees.|A seven story 150 unit apartment building took severe roof damage in Lansdale Borough, Montgomery County. The Montgomery County Community Based COVID-19 Testing Site shut down at noon due to the storms. Personnel took shelter and reported minor property damage.||Because this derecho moved off the coast by 130 PM, the warm afternoon sun was able to sufficiently destabilize the atmosphere for the formation of another round of severe thunderstorms over some of the same areas that experience them earlier in the day.  Reported wind gusts associated with these thunderstorms generally ranged between 45 and 65 MPH, with a 65 MPH wind gust measured at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The remaining thunderstorms then moved offshore by 1000 PM, which brought an end to the day's severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees and power lines down in the Collegeville area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate severe weather events affected central Pennsylvania on June 3, 2020. The first event occurred during the pre-dawn hours, as strong thunderstorms pushed southward out of the Buffalo, NY area into the northwest mountains and produced wind damage before dissipating. The second event comprised of an MCS that dropped quickly southeastward across the area during the late morning hours and produced occasional wind damage along it's path. The third event saw scattered thunderstorms, including one long-lived supercell, develop during the afternoon hours and drift southeastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down several large trees and damaged a garage near Dover.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down trees, blocking a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed on the higher terrain of the Allegheny Front during the early afternoon hours and drifted eastward through the afternoon and early evening in a moderate CAPE, moderate low-level shear environment well ahead of an approaching trough of low pressure over the Great Lakes. A few of these storms produced wind damage, along with an isolated report of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated at 60 mph downed multiple trees in State College, including a tree along the 1100 block of West Beaver Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis lifted quickly across the Great Lakes and the Upper Ohio Valley on November 15th, taking on a negative tilt as it did so. Deepening surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into Ontario during the day, whipping a cold front across the region during the late morning and early afternoon. Some of the classic ingredients for a widespread wind event were present, including a negatively- tilted trough, jet positioning over the region, and a favorable Great Lakes surface low track. Strong post-frontal 3 hour pressure rises of 5-7 mb were observed, and a low-topped mostly lightning-free convective line accompanied the boundary. Widespread wind gusts of at least 45-55 MPH were observed, with several higher measured gusts seen. Along the convective line, gusts of 55-65 MPH likely occurred. This resulted in widespread reports of wind damage to trees and power lines, with a few instances of mostly minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor structural damage occurred to barn doors and fencing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate severe weather events affected central Pennsylvania on June 3, 2020. The first event occurred during the pre-dawn hours, as strong thunderstorms pushed southward out of the Buffalo, NY area into the northwest mountains and produced wind damage before dissipating. The second event comprised of an MCS that dropped quickly southeastward across the area during the late morning hours and produced occasional wind damage along it's path. The third event saw scattered thunderstorms, including one long-lived supercell, develop during the afternoon hours and drift southeastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto wires in Red Lion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis lifted quickly across the Great Lakes and the Upper Ohio Valley on November 15th, taking on a negative tilt as it did so. Deepening surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into Ontario during the day, whipping a cold front across the region during the late morning and early afternoon. Some of the classic ingredients for a widespread wind event were present, including a negatively- tilted trough, jet positioning over the region, and a favorable Great Lakes surface low track. Strong post-frontal 3 hour pressure rises of 5-7 mb were observed, and a low-topped mostly lightning-free convective line accompanied the boundary. Widespread wind gusts of at least 45-55 MPH were observed, with several higher measured gusts seen. Along the convective line, gusts of 55-65 MPH likely occurred. This resulted in widespread reports of wind damage to trees and power lines, with a few instances of mostly minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to be blown down on to a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Narrow lines of low-topped convection were able to tap into strong winds just off the surface to produce wind damage across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of November 15, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in several locations near Somerset, including on the 900 block of New Centerville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate severe weather events affected central Pennsylvania on June 3, 2020. The first event occurred during the pre-dawn hours, as strong thunderstorms pushed southward out of the Buffalo, NY area into the northwest mountains and produced wind damage before dissipating. The second event comprised of an MCS that dropped quickly southeastward across the area during the late morning hours and produced occasional wind damage along it's path. The third event saw scattered thunderstorms, including one long-lived supercell, develop during the afternoon hours and drift southeastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Troxelville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers changed to snow on the evening of November 1st, behind a cold front that brought an early shot of cold air to western Pennsylvania. Northwest flow, which helped to provide enhancement to the snow from Lake Erie, kept snow showers going through most of the following night, with some accumulations reported.  An enhanced band that extended into Mercer County was responsible for the event's highest snow totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was reported at Tionesta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate severe weather events affected central Pennsylvania on June 3, 2020. The first event occurred during the pre-dawn hours, as strong thunderstorms pushed southward out of the Buffalo, NY area into the northwest mountains and produced wind damage before dissipating. The second event comprised of an MCS that dropped quickly southeastward across the area during the late morning hours and produced occasional wind damage along it's path. The third event saw scattered thunderstorms, including one long-lived supercell, develop during the afternoon hours and drift southeastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Berrysburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 10th led to humid and very warm conditions.  Mean-layer CAPE of around 2000 J/kg, highest over eastern Ohio, illustrated the very unstable conditions.  A few discrete thunderstorm cells were able to form over southwest PA initially, with a few hail and wind damage reports.  However, the bulk of the severe weather was the result of the passage of a negatively-tilted upper trough and a strong cold front, which crossed during the late afternoon and evening hours. Bulk shear of 40 to 50 knots and the cold front allowed for the development of a quasi-linear convective system, resulting in widespread reports of wind damage.  Gusts of 70-80 MPH were estimated in some cases, leading to numerous reports of damage to trees and power lines, as well as mostly minor structural damage to roofing and shingles. Mesovortices in the QLCS also led to the development and confirmation of three tornadoes in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area. The strongest was in Beaver County PA, with EF1 damage observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five large trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crossing shortwave trough, as well as a remnant mesoscale convective vortex from convection the previous night, acted as a trigger for another round of severe thunderstorms on the 4th of June. Surface-based CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 30-40 knots set up the region for organized severe weather.  Storms initiated in central Ohio around midday and then congealed into a line which swept across the upper Ohio Valley through the middle to late afternoon hours. Severe weather reports were mainly limited to tree and power line damage, with only a few reports of damage to structures and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree was reported to be blocking all lanes on Bower Hill Road by Painters Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms were able to form in an environment with 1000-2000 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE.  Synoptic support was relatively weak, keeping storms from becoming organized. However, the entrainment of dry mid-level air made 700-1000 J/kg of downdraft CAPE available, which led to several instances of downburst wind in the stronger storms.  Several wind damage reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be blown down along Valley View Road in Nottingham Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms were able to form along a boundary left by the passage of a decaying mesoscale convective system during the morning hours of the 27th.  Dry air aloft led to the development of sufficient downdraft CAPE, allowing a few instances of localized strong wind.  A few reports of flooding were received as well, as storms were able to train east-west along the boundary and create localized heavy rainfall totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to be rushing across US 422 adjacent to the BP gas station on the east side of Shelocta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crossing shortwave trough, as well as a remnant mesoscale convective vortex from convection the previous night, acted as a trigger for another round of severe thunderstorms on the 4th of June. Surface-based CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 30-40 knots set up the region for organized severe weather.  Storms initiated in central Ohio around midday and then congealed into a line which swept across the upper Ohio Valley through the middle to late afternoon hours. Severe weather reports were mainly limited to tree and power line damage, with only a few reports of damage to structures and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto the road, blocking a lane of travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 10th led to humid and very warm conditions.  Mean-layer CAPE of around 2000 J/kg, highest over eastern Ohio, illustrated the very unstable conditions.  A few discrete thunderstorm cells were able to form over southwest PA initially, with a few hail and wind damage reports.  However, the bulk of the severe weather was the result of the passage of a negatively-tilted upper trough and a strong cold front, which crossed during the late afternoon and evening hours. Bulk shear of 40 to 50 knots and the cold front allowed for the development of a quasi-linear convective system, resulting in widespread reports of wind damage.  Gusts of 70-80 MPH were estimated in some cases, leading to numerous reports of damage to trees and power lines, as well as mostly minor structural damage to roofing and shingles. Mesovortices in the QLCS also led to the development and confirmation of three tornadoes in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area. The strongest was in Beaver County PA, with EF1 damage observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported down in the area of New Castle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage started in the vicinity of Tuscarawas road then moved northeast to where five power poles were snapped near the Shop N' Save plaza. This is where the |strongest damage was observed of the entire track, with an estimated peak wind speed of 105-110 MPH. For the remainder of the track, hardwood and softwood trees were used to estimate wind speed. ||The tornado continued northeast and caused uprooted hardwood and|softwood trees through Brady's Run Park. A lull in damage was|observed given limited access around Interstate 376 south of the |Brighton exit. Damage picked up again in Daughtery township where |at least 40 to 50 hardwood trees were snapped or uprooted. One |tree that was uprooted left a six foot crater in the ground. The |last damage was recorded just northeast of Klein Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms were able to form in an environment with 1000-2000 J/kg of mean-layer CAPE.  Synoptic support was relatively weak, keeping storms from becoming organized. However, the entrainment of dry mid-level air made 700-1000 J/kg of downdraft CAPE available, which led to several instances of downburst wind in the stronger storms.  Several wind damage reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires and trees were reported to be down near Robinson Road in Cokeburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crossing shortwave trough led to the development of thunderstorms across the region during the midday and afternoon hours of the 22nd.  Much of the region was impacted by cloud cover early on and was not able to destabilize sufficiently for severe weather.  However, areas to the north and east of Pittsburgh, where more sun was seen and where the trough had yet to cross, saw thunderstorms take advantage of the synoptic support, instability and terrain influences. Indiana and Jefferson Counties took the brunt of the wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of trees were down between Vintondale and Wehrum.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This path was evaluated owing to the presence of a radar-depicted|mesovortex track. Damage along this extensive track was the most|considerable examined today, with abundant tree destruction in the|form of uprooted or snapped hardwood trunks, widespread snapped|large limbs and occasional fascia and shingle damage to single-|family residences. A tree was blown onto an occupied vehicle east|of Rogers, OH early in the swath before wind speed appeared to|increase considerably. Elsewhere along the path, several |outbuildings suffered roofing damage, including a barn in Lawrence|County, where a portion of the roofing structure was removed and |transported downwind among other local damage. Damage to power|poles along the swath appears tied to trees falling on power|lines.||Damage was concentrated in several areas, including the western|and northern sections of East Palestine, the area near Howell |Road/Route 14 area and areas adjacent to the Pennsylvania Turnpike|in Little Beaver and North Beaver Townships of Lawrence County,|PA.||Ultimately this damage was deemed to be non-tornadic but of|considerable intensity. The swath generally paralleled the radar-|depicted mesovortex on its southeast flank, but featured |considerable width and a modestly divergent pattern atypical of|mesovortex tornadoes. Nevertheless, the wind speed along this|swath was of comparable intensity to many tornadoes and over a |broader area, leading to the widespread damage observed.||At its peak over this roughly 11 mile swath examined, damage was|consistent with speed up to 80 mph at times. A vast majority of|the damage examined was consistent with wind of around 70 mph.||It is entirely probable that severe wind continued northeast of|the swath evaluated today, but a coherent swath was no longer|noted, and the radar-depicted mesovortex dissipated hereafter.

Storms initiated in central Ohio around midday and then congealed into a line which swept across the upper Ohio Valley through the middle to late afternoon hours. Severe weather reports were mainly limited to tree and power line damage, with only a few reports of damage to structures and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto power lines in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crossing shortwave trough led to the development of thunderstorms across the region during the midday and afternoon hours of the 22nd.  Much of the region was impacted by cloud cover early on and was not able to destabilize sufficiently for severe weather.  However, areas to the north and east of Pittsburgh, where more sun was seen and where the trough had yet to cross, saw thunderstorms take advantage of the synoptic support, instability and terrain influences. Indiana and Jefferson Counties took the brunt of the wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees caused damage to power lines and cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 10th led to humid and very warm conditions.  Mean-layer CAPE of around 2000 J/kg, highest over eastern Ohio, illustrated the very unstable conditions.  A few discrete thunderstorm cells were able to form over southwest PA initially, with a few hail and wind damage reports.  However, the bulk of the severe weather was the result of the passage of a negatively-tilted upper trough and a strong cold front, which crossed during the late afternoon and evening hours. Bulk shear of 40 to 50 knots and the cold front allowed for the development of a quasi-linear convective system, resulting in widespread reports of wind damage.  Gusts of 70-80 MPH were estimated in some cases, leading to numerous reports of damage to trees and power lines, as well as mostly minor structural damage to roofing and shingles. Mesovortices in the QLCS also led to the development and confirmation of three tornadoes in the NWS Pittsburgh forecast area. The strongest was in Beaver County PA, with EF1 damage observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in the area around Osceola Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm passed through the region December 1st through December 2nd and created up to a foot of snow in some portions of our region.||The low pressure system advanced through PA and NY. Deep moisture and lift resulted in snow across the Upper Ohio Valley region. Lake enhancement and upsloping also helped with high snowfall totals. During the storm, 850mb temps lingered near -9C and the dendritic growth zone was about 10,000 feet above the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell in 12 hours in Armstrong County. Highest amount recorded in Freeport, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure began to develop just off the coast of the Carolinas early on December 16, and steadily moved up the coast through the 16th and into the 17th. The low steadily, but not rapidly, intensified as it moved north. Plentiful moisture was associated with the low thanks to a Gulf of Mexico connection. In addition, very strong high pressure was centered just north of New England, which helped lock in cold air over the mid-Atlantic. The combination of the moisture-rich low and cold air in place led to a significant winter storm over much of the mid-Atlantic. Heavy snow and sleet fell over the area, with some areas also changing to rain as warmer ocean air surged inland and warmer air moved in aloft. While the central pressure of the storm system was not especially low (around 995 mb) the gradient between the low pressure and the very strong high to the north also led to strong winds in the region, especially near the coast. Combined with high astronomical tides, this also led to some coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches were observed, including a report of 6.8 inches from a trained spotter in Chadds Ford Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter moved slowly up the US eastern coastline on the afternoon of the 16th through the 17th. The storm system produced very heavy snowfall across parts of Central New York and Northeast Pennsylvania, particularly during the very early morning hours of the 17th. The highest snowfall totals were found across the Northern Tier of Pennsylvania to the Southern Tier of New York to the Catskills, where 30 to 40+ inches of snow fell. Extreme snowfall rates of 5-6 inches per hour were reported at times in the heaviest parts of the band. Transportation impacts were extensive with numerous road closures and delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 15 to 18 inches.

The highest snowfall totals were found across the Northern Tier of Pennsylvania to the Southern Tier of New York to the Catskills, where 30 to 40+ inches of snow fell. Extreme snowfall rates of 5-6 inches per hour were reported at times in the heaviest parts of the band. Transportation impacts were extensive with numerous road closures and delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 20 to 43 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough combined with a coupled jet structure resulted in an impressive snow storm across the region. The resulting divergence aloft generated strong frontogenetically forced snow bands across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow fell in 24 hours. Highest amount in Hermitage, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure began to develop just off the coast of the Carolinas early on December 16, and steadily moved up the coast through the 16th and into the 17th. The low steadily, but not rapidly, intensified as it moved north. Plentiful moisture was associated with the low thanks to a Gulf of Mexico connection. In addition, very strong high pressure was centered just north of New England, which helped lock in cold air over the mid-Atlantic. The combination of the moisture-rich low and cold air in place led to a significant winter storm over much of the mid-Atlantic. Heavy snow and sleet fell over the area, with some areas also changing to rain as warmer ocean air surged inland and warmer air moved in aloft. While the central pressure of the storm system was not especially low (around 995 mb) the gradient between the low pressure and the very strong high to the north also led to strong winds in the region, especially near the coast. Combined with high astronomical tides, this also led to some coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the county, with amounts of 10 to 15 inches observed. A trained spotter in East Stroudsburg reported 15.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought rain transitioning to snow into the region December 24-25th. The deep and pivoting trough maintained the resident time of the precipitation and supported robust snowfall rates.  Snow accumulation by 7 AM on December 25th provided an official white Christmas across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4.5 inches fell at the NWS Pittsburgh office on Christmas morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm passed through the region December 1st through December 2nd and created up to a foot of snow in some portions of our region.||The low pressure system advanced through PA and NY. Deep moisture and lift resulted in snow across the Upper Ohio Valley region. Lake enhancement and upsloping also helped with high snowfall totals. During the storm, 850mb temps lingered near -9C and the dendritic growth zone was about 10,000 feet above the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On December 1st 6 to 7 inches of snow fell in about 12 hours in portions of Indiana County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season Nor'Easter produced anywhere from 6 inches of snow and sleet in the Lower Susquehanna Valley up to 30 inches of snow in parts of Tioga County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 9 to 13 inches of snow across Blair County from December 16-17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 21, 2020 in a low-CAPE, and relatively modest shear environment. These storms produced sporadic wind damage as they pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down several trees onto Church Drive to the south of Windber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moderate CAPE/weak shear environment during the early evening hours of July 8, 2020. A multi-cell cluster produced wind damage in Sullivan County, and few pulse storms produced wind damage in the Susquehanna Valley.  ||Slow moving thunderstorms also brought isolated flash flooding to Somerset and York Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree across Route 200 near Laporte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 21, 2020 in a low-CAPE, and relatively modest shear environment. These storms produced sporadic wind damage as they pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm microburst produced straight line wind damage near the town of Ashtola, Pennsylvania, which is about 4 miles SE of Windber. Approximately 75-100 trees on private property just west of the Gallitzin State Forest and east of Crum Rd were downed by strong winds. Most of the damage was confined to an area about 100 yards wide and 500 yards long. Winds were estimated at 85-95 mph. We thank Somerset County and Ogle Township Emergency Management for providing aerial imagery for the damage analysis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 21, 2020 in a low-CAPE, and relatively modest shear environment. These storms produced sporadic wind damage as they pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm microburst produced straight line wind damage in Fayetteville, PA. Approximately 100 trees were downed by strong winds along a path approximately 700 yards long and 100 yards wide. Most of this damage occurred in a forested area alongside the road between 1640 and 1350 Woodstock Rd. In addition to this concentrated swath of damage, peripheral wind damage was scattered between Fayetteville and Chambersburg, including several isolated instances of trees down and damage to roofs of buildings. Wind in this area was estimated at 70-80 mph. A roof was ripped off a warehouse building on the 1600 block of Lincoln Highway. Another roof was partially removed from a building on the 1700 block of Lincoln Highway. A roof was partially removed from a barn and a shed was destroyed on the 2500 block of Grant Point Rd. One person was injured when a tree fell onto a person in Chambersburg. We thank the Civil Air Patrol for providing aerial imagery that was used in this damage analysis.
